data.title: Alvotech: Improving Fundamentals Signal A Potential Bottom For This Biosimilar Player
data.description:
Alvotech (ALVO) is an Icelandic biotech company focusing on the development and manufacturing of biosimilar medicines. To scale its commercialization of its products, it partners with global pharmaceutical companies, such as Teva or Advanz Pharma, where it could then generate revenues from the shares of the sales of its products and milestone-based fees. ALVO primarily competes with the likes of Swiss-based Sandoz or Samsung Bioepis, which also operate within the biosimilar segment.

Since going public, share performance has been underwhelming and volatile. The stock is down around -63% from an all-time standpoint, while it is also down -59% over the past year alone, currently trading at merely around $4 per share range.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $4 presents over 12% upside from the current price of $3.6 per share.

Financial Reviews

Overall, fundamentals appear decent as of Q4 2025, marked by double-digit revenue growth and positive GAAP profitability.

While ALVO delivered a Q4 revenue of $173 million, which is only a 13% YoY growth, the company actually clocked in a 21% YoY growth on an annual basis with $593 million FY revenue, which points to stronger commercial execution.

Likewise, we also see an overall improvement in profitability. For instance, GAAP net profitability turned positive, though it seems to mainly be driven by a non-cash item. The key highlight is in the adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA), which saw a 27% YoY increase to $137 million, an important milestone in operating profitability performance.

Liquidity and cash-flow situations are generally where ALVO seems to be lagging behind as of FY 2025. ALVO ended FY 2025 with $172 million of cash, a decent jump from last year’s $51 million. Yet, it mainly relied on financing cash flow from net borrowings rather than operating cash flow (OCF). Though OCF was positive $7 million in FY 2025, ALVO’s aggressive investments into its operations and also sizable interest payments on its current $1.45 billion of debt mean that it will need to rely on further borrowings to sustain its operations, until the company can consistently deliver strong aEBITDA and OCFs to be self-sustaining.

Catalyst

Overall, I believe catalysts remain moderate to high for ALVO. Given the fact that the management has shared their 2026 outlook, it is crucial to break down their assumptions and then analyze them separately, in my opinion.

First off, the FY 2026 revenue forecast, which aims for 14% YoY revenue growth, suggests that it may be driven partially by the FDA approval of the 4 major Biologics License Applications (BLA), though the impact will be minimal since it is only happening late in 2026.

With that in mind, we should expect a lot of the revenue growth to come from the continued commercialization of existing products or new geographic expansions. Looking at the pipeline, I think that the management’s targets seem realistic and reasonable, since there is still plenty of upside here, in my opinion.

Selarsdi alone, which is a Stelara biosimilar, for instance, could be a meaningful growth lever, in my view, especially in the US market, where ALVO already partnered with Teva to drive adoption:

Stelara represents another large and attractive biologics market with significant biosimilar opportunity, and we continue to see strong rollout of AVT04 across key regions. In the United States, where biosimilars now account for approximately 40% of the market, Teva continues to expand formulary coverage for Selarsdi, holding a strong and growing market position.

Source: Q4 earnings call

My estimation suggests that its reference drug, Stelara, could still be doing around $7 billion in sales in the US alone in 2026, based on its peak revenue of $11 billion in 2023 and the assumption of around $1.1 billion of consistent annual decline, as per the article's report, from the peak figure.

Therefore, a modest revenue capture here may be material to support revenue growth. Meanwhile, I also expect that the rest of ALVO’s promising portfolio of biosimilars will continue to contribute meaningfully to revenue growth due to its large market size. Other than Selarsdi, AVT02, which is a Humira biosimilar, is also already scaling across key markets like the US, where its reference drug, Humira, held 70% market share at one point in 2025 pre-biosimilar launch.

Risk

I believe risk remains relatively high. Looking at the overall trading performance of ALVO over the past few years, it has become clear that the stock performance seems to be influenced strongly by the regulatory approvals. As such, despite the relatively compelling biosimilar pipeline and obviously interesting growth opportunities coming from expiring patents in the next few years, ALVO seems to offer low margins of error.

For instance, any further regulatory delays due to unresolved submission to address the outstanding July 2025 FDA issues in FY 2026 could potentially result in correction due to lack of positive sentiment on the stock:

In the fourth quarter of 2025, we received complete response letters from the FDA for these applications. Further, after receiving the CRLs, we received a post application action letter, or PAAL, detailing the remaining open items with the FDA after review of our 483 response. The CRLs were related to issues identified following the FDA's inspection of our Reykjavik facility in July of 2025.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Of course, on the other hand, I believe that ALVO’s high leverage is definitely another risk factor worth mentioning, since primarily, it exposes the company to interest rate volatility. As I mentioned earlier in the financial review section, ALVO has about $1.45 billion in debt, with a net debt to aEBITDA ratio of 9.3x. Though ALVO expects to bring it down to over 6x in FY 2026, the number remains quite high, and I think this situation may continue to put a ceiling on its valuation premium.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for ALVO is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the management’s target:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions - I project ALVO to successfully clear FDA issues anytime in 2026, delivering the high-end of its revenue guidance, driven by Selarsdi and also AVT02 sales, and reaching its target margins and leverage. I apply a 2x P/S multiple, higher than its TTM P/S, which suggests a market re-rating.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions - I expect FDA delays to impact the launch of potential 4 BLAs, which further dims market sentiment on ALVO due to the lack of catalysts going beyond FY 2026. ALVO misses its low-end revenue guidance and struggles to bring down its high leverage, followed by the discounted 1.2x P/S multiple.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $4 per share, presenting a potential upside of over 12% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Overall, my price target analysis relies on the 60-40 bull-bear probability assumption. The fact that the management comes out and provides double-digit revenue guidance as well as good enough clarity on its other financial expectations and pipeline, for me, gives enough confidence to lean more on the bullish side of things.

Conclusion

ALVO’s fundamentals in FY 2025 have improved quite significantly, as marked by double digit growth and positive GAAP net profitability. While ALVO’s high leverage and minimal OCF generation have been a good start, in my view. In 2026, there are some potential opportunities to capture that should help the company to maintain a similar performance, and if the company could execute well, I believe a re-rating is more than possible. The main risk remains the potential delay in FDA approval, where any regulatory decision going against ALVO could surely lead to a sizable correction. Overall, the view remains slightly bullish for me, and at 12% projected upside, I think ALVO presents a decent risk-reward for a speculative buy, as it may be close to bottoming out.

data.title: Microsoft: Temporary CapEx Panic Masks The Long-Term Edge
data.description:
I rate Microsoft (MSFT) a strong buy at $374.33 (at the time of writing). Wall Street is mispricing Microsoft’s shift into an agentic business leader and punishing MSFT stock for a temporary $37.5 billion CapEx cycle and FCF compression. This panic does not consider the depreciation gap, as aggressive hardware amortization masks a big possible operating leverage (the outcome). As Microsoft shifts from a cloud provider to the definitive AI control plane, the launch of the M365 E7 Frontier Suite ($99/user/month) can absorb shrinking enterprise SaaS budgets. Further, shifting inferencing workloads to its own Maia 200 silicon defends the 65% Azure gross margin floor over the long term against Nvidia's (NVDA) pricing power. However, the main risks to my strong buy MSFT stock thesis are the $281 billion OpenAI (OPENAI) concentration risk [that is mainly OpenAI routing future workloads to Amazon (AMZN) AWS] and the Headless Office phenomenon (autonomous AI agents can lead to high corporate seat deflation) that is risking Microsoft’s core per-user subscription annuity model.

My previous coverage/thesis rated Microsoft stock as a strong buy based on its shift into an irreplaceable AI utility provider. The bullish outlook was based on a big surge in Q1 commercial bookings and Remaining Performance Obligation [RPO] backlog. This pre-sold order book trend, in my opinion (at that time), backs high revenue projections and is a buffer against macro shocks, and it is fueling Azure's growth. That thesis marked short-term risks from the CapEx depreciation cliff linked to short-lived AI hardware. The article’s blended valuation model projected up to a 113% mid-to-long-term price upside (target of $608–$722 range). I marked, for a worst-case price drop, $388 as a major long-term support at that time, and now MSFT stock is near this level. In the current article, I have updated the thesis for the upcoming Q3 results with an expanded analysis of MSFT stock.

What Can Take Microsoft's Stock Price Higher?

Ok, so, on the positives, I want to start with Microsoft’s Agentic OS leadership and move to monetizing the 'SaaSapocalypse.' Wall Street, in my opinion, fears that AI will cannibalize the SaaS industry and drive enterprise spending down. But logically, Microsoft is not blind to this risk, and as per my observations, it is engineering this destruction to its advantage. To clarify more, Microsoft is shifting its target battleground from individual model intelligence to multi-agent orchestration. With the launch of Agent 365 and the M365 E7 tier ($99/user/month), MSFT has formed the control plane for the enterprise. AI models are becoming commoditized, but the enterprise data graph is not. On this trend, Work IQ ingests the entirety of an enterprise’s unstructured data (emails, Teams transcripts, SharePoint docs). When a company builds custom agents through Microsoft Foundry or Copilot Studio (already used by 80% of the Fortune 500), those agents must route through Work IQ to access enterprise context. As a result, Microsoft is building an inescapable toll on the enterprise AI road. In this, the value capture happens as enterprises cancel point-solution SaaS subscriptions (CRM, ERP, HR software) to use custom-built agentic workflows. Along with that, those recovered IT budgets will flow into Microsoft's E7 licenses and Azure compute consumption, and through this, Microsoft is eating the larger SaaS TAM.

Apart from this, there is CapEx de-risking and the Custom Silicon Total Cost of Ownership [TCO] arbitrage for Microsoft. Wall Street considers $37.5 billion in Q2-FY2026 CapEx (two-thirds on short-lived GPUs/CPUs) and panics over depreciation and ROI. Sharp bulls need to understand Microsoft’s hardware lifecycle economics here. Microsoft’s Commercial bookings growth is at 230% Y/Y, and Commercial RPO is at $625 billion (+110% Y/Y) with an extended duration of 2.5 years. What's important here is that the capacity Microsoft is deploying is largely forward-contracted. More on this: Microsoft brings the Maia 200 accelerator online that is delivering 10+ petaFLOPS at FP4 precision with a 30% improved TCO over previous fleets. Moreover, inferencing for agentic models needs intense CPU usage alongside GPUs. For that, the new Cobalt 200 CPU works at 50% higher performance. As Microsoft shifts its internal inferencing (Copilot, GitHub) and Foundry workloads to its own silicon stack, gross margins (that dipped to 67%) can aggressively expand, along with reducing its margin dependency on Nvidia’s pricing power.

In addition to these catalysts, Microsoft Foundry is commoditizing the Foundation Model layer. In my stance, Microsoft’s move on Microsoft Foundry is brilliant as it is commoditizing the LLM layer to capture the infrastructure margin. To expand more here, I mean bringing Fireworks AI to Foundry and hosting leading open/custom models (DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2.5, MiniMax M2.5). Microsoft provides day-zero high-throughput inference for non-OpenAI models. So through that, Microsoft is actively decoupling the ‘harness’ (orchestration/Foundry) from the ‘model’ (OpenAI). If an enterprise wants to use Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude 4.6/a custom-quantized DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) model, it can do so smoothly on Azure. Therefore, here, Microsoft secures the cloud revenue irrespective of which foundation model wins the benchmark wars. This factor is also hedging Microsoft’s OpenAI counterparty risk.

With all that, Microsoft is building the infrastructure for Physical AI and sovereign national security. In the last few months, Microsoft has earmarked $10 billion for Japan, $5.5 billion for Singapore, and $1 billion for Thailand to build in-country AI infrastructure. In my opinion, through guaranteeing local data residency and integrating with local telecom/government grids, Microsoft is locking out AWS and Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) from Asian sovereign AI contracts. All in all, as of now, digital AI is a saturated market, and physical AI (robotics, autonomous systems) is the next trillion-dollar market. Microsoft is deepening integration between Fabric, Foundry, and NVIDIA Omniverse that allows industrial giants [like BMW (BMWKY) and Unilever (UL)] to run physics-accurate digital twins connected to live operational data. This needs exponentially more compute than text generation. Thus, it is opening up big and sticky industrial CapEx cycles.

What Can Take Microsoft's Stock Price Lower?

To start on the negatives, I want to point out the OpenAI ‘Trojan Horse’ and the $281 Billion Concentration risk first. Microsoft’s most potent asset is fastly mutating into a systemic problem. More on this, 45% of Microsoft's $625 billion RPO ($281 Billion) is linked to OpenAI. As Wall Street knows, OpenAI is severely cash-burning. Due to this, OpenAI raised $110 billion that is heavily backed by Amazon (AMZN) ($50 billion) and Nvidia ($30 billion). In my opinion, it is bad for Microsoft’s exclusive advantage that Amazon AWS secured the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider status for OpenAI Frontier. If OpenAI begins shifting its training and inference workloads to AWS Trainium4 clusters to back its Amazon deal, Microsoft’s $281 billion contracted backlog becomes a phantom topline. Although MSFT is entitled to have 20% of OpenAI’s revenue through 2032, the renegotiated terms removed Microsoft’s first right of refusal. This is allowing OpenAI to court Microsoft's direct competitors. If OpenAI ticks AGI (or claims to), the existing commercial agreements may become void, and this may possibly leave Microsoft with a $150 billion+ depreciating hardware fleet and no foundation model (proprietary) advantage.

Beyond that, I am not ignoring the ‘Headless Office’ and Seat Deflation problem. As per CEO Satya Nadella, "The next Office... may be headless." In this quote, I see a fatal risk to Microsoft’s most sacred cash cow. To answer why I see that, Microsoft currently holds 450 million+ M365 commercial seats. However, as autonomous agents (as digital workers) replace human knowledge workers, corporate headcount can shrink. In my stance, the shift from Per-User/Per-Month licensing [SaaS] to Consumption-Based Pricing (Tokens/Compute) is highly volatile. For example, if an enterprise replaces 10,000 human analysts ($35/mo E5 license) with 500 autonomous AI agents running on background APIs, Microsoft’s predictable annuity revenue model can drop theoretically. Microsoft 365 revenue growth (17% Y/Y, as of Q2) can decelerate into the single digits (in a worst-case scenario) as the white-collar workforce contracts.

Apart from that, there is the power generation mandate that can lead to a Utility-Multiple downgrade for MSFT stock. The AI market expansion has collided with the limitations of the physical world. Datacenters may account for a projected 12% of all US electricity by 2028. The US Administration has mandated that hyperscalers must derive their own dedicated power for expanding AI infrastructure (instead of draining local grids). The argument is that if Microsoft is forced to build/buy/finance nuclear/SMR/natural gas power plants (like the 900MW Crusoe lease in Abilene, Texas), its capital intensity may permanently alter its financial profile. A long-term shift from high-margin software to heavy industrial power generation can force Wall Street to re-rate MSFT stock downward from a software multiple (22x FWD P/E) to a lower industrial/utility multiple (or at the midpoint of both)

Lastly, Microsoft is deploying ~$37.5 billion a quarter (as of Q2). Two-thirds of this is on short-lived assets (like GPUs). Hardware decay is currently in the Nvidia Blackwell/Rubin generation. However, because inference economics demands extreme memory bandwidth and low latency, hardware is aging faster than its accounting depreciation schedule (currently modeled over 6 years). If newer and cheaper architectures (like Grok's SRAM-heavy inference chips/highly optimized ASICs) become the industry standard for agentic workloads by 2027, Microsoft may be forced to take huge write-downs on its older GPU fleets. If revenue growth from M365 Copilot (currently at a modest 15 million paid seats, translating to only ~3.3% penetration of the 450 million base) stalls, the FCF margin (already projected to compress toward the low 20s) can temporarily become negative, which has not happened in decades. Therefore, it is too bad for MSFT stock valuation. To answer why? Because I am observing Wall Street’s high sensitivity to a decline in FCF to Equity [FCFE] margin pace (quarterly against TTM %).

Microsoft Stock's Long-Term Valuation

To value Microsoft logically, I segregated the divergence between scaling profitability and dropping FCF. As of Q2, Microsoft has a ($37.5 billion*4) $150 billion annualized run rate. As a result, the TTM Levered FCF margin has compressed to 17.56%, and BNP Paribas estimates FCF margins will go in the low 20s (down from ~30% in FY24). Despite the cash drain, Microsoft just posted Q2 EPS of $4.14 (+24% Y/Y). To answer, how? Depreciation Accounting. Microsoft capitalizes its servers over a 6-year useful life, and as observed in the ongoing Michael Burry debate, the massive $381 billion spent by hyperscalers in 2025 is being depreciated over extended periods that are inflating reported operating income by upwards of 20% in the short term. If Microsoft was building data centers on speculation, then this may be a catastrophic risk. But it is not; now I am observing that Microsoft’s Commercial RPO has an average duration of 2.5 years, along with, as per CFO Amy Hood, the GPUs being purchased are “already contracted for the entirety of their useful life.” The thing is that Wall Street is punishing MSFT stock for FCF compression and treating it as a cash-incinerating hardware utility. But the capital is fully de-risked by the $625 billion RPO. As these assets age, their utility improves; that may be driving high gross margin leverage in Years 3-6 of the hardware lifecycle.

Back to valuations, first, I am using Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] Analysis to capture the reality of the CapEx cycle. I am projecting out to FY30 (June 2030), mapping the consensus revenue estimates against the FCF margin drop and recovery. I am assuming a 30% FCF margin, as it is the 10-year long-term average. To answer why? I am marking year-wise reasons. FY2026 is the trough phase: Hyper-aggressive GPU/datacenter buildout; FY2027: Peak CapEx intensity before $200 billion plateau; FY2028: Inference efficiency gains through Maia 200/Cobalt 200; FY2029: CapEx growth slows, Agentic high-margin software revenues compound; and FY2030: Return to normalized peak AI-software-like cash flow. On a cash-based analysis, punishing Microsoft for its current CapEx spend, the stock appears overvalued at $374.33 against a DCF Equity Value Per Share of $360.33. However, this is the exact trap smart bulls avoid. The DCF model fails to capture the 6-year utility and monetization curve of the hardware beyond the 5-year forecast window. Now, to derive the true bias-clearing price, I am assessing MSFT’s earnings power and multiple expansion possibilities.

In my opinion, Wall Street may not continue to price hyperscalers through FCF swings. It can also price mature tech giants on Forward EPS and structural moats. So secondly, I use forward EPS consensus with the Multiple compression > expansion argument. MSFT traded at 32x+ forward earnings. But today, it trades at 22.4x FY2026 EPS and 19.9x FY2027 EPS. Wall Street has derated MSFT stock (as per Wells Fargo's Oh Sung Kwon, an extreme derating) to treat it like a capital-intensive utility instead of a high-margin software business.

I am considering 28x as the logical long-term earnings multiple. Why will the multiple expand back to 28x? Two things: one is Silicon Independence (Maia and Cobalt), and the second is Fabric and Foundry Monetization. Fabric is already at a $2 billion ARR (+60% Y/Y), and Microsoft Foundry is hosting 1,500+ clients spending $1M+/quarter. Thus, this is a high-margin orchestration revenue that sits on top of the compute layer. If I apply a conservative 28x forward P/E to FY2030 EPS of $31.84 and derive its present value [PV] at a 10.5132% cost of equity (source: GuruFocus), I get a $584.25 current target price for MSFT Stock. To attain the final target price, I blend the FCF reality [DCF] with the Earnings Power reality [PE]. Along with that, if I apply the OpenAI Concentration Risk Discount at 5% (hypothetical), the risk-adjusted blended valuation model yields a current value of $448.68 per share for MSFT stock (+19.86%, immediate price upside).

Microsoft Stock Technical Analysis

On the technical side, the weekly price chart of Microsoft stock indicates that MSFT is an asset in the late stages of a sharp macro-distribution cycle. MSFT stock has corrected from its late-2025 peak to its current price level of $374.33. MSFT stock price is now near the S1 at $375.18 (that is the first 2026 Fibonacci support level). Moreover, I am observing a sell-side-exhaustion and momentum-reversal setup as the core oscillators are now at historical depths. At the same time, these oscillators are indicating early-stage bullish reversal signals at a main Fibonacci support floor [S1] in line with the DCF-based price floor for MSFT. In the price, there is the polarity formation. To clarify more here, in the price pattern from late-2021 to mid-2023, the $350 resistance was a big accumulation/reversal level in late-2023 and early-2025. After that, MSFT stock scaled to the ATH in late 2025. So, MSFT's stock price’s prior resistance was shifted into important support. FYI, the current retesting is the exact base of the previous bull market.

Apart from that, the 5-week EMA is at $378.17, and the 13-week EMA (13-week) is at $402.39. The 5-week EMA is below the 13-week EMA, which is confirming the leading short-term price downtrend. The rate of MSFT stock price decline is decelerating. And with that, the expanding spread between these EMAs signals that the pace of the selling pressure is going away. Therefore, these EMAs are providing space (least resistance) for reversal. Initially, a considerable break and weekly close above the EMA (5) can trigger a sell-position-covering trend, in my opinion. This may then drive MSFT stock price toward the $403 level (the primary reversion target).

Moreover, the core oscillators indicate an important bullish divergence/reversal state. The Stochastic oscillator is buried in a sub-10 oversold state. Double bottom markings below 10 are exceedingly rare for a mega-cap tech like MSFT and indicate a pure buying window. The fast line (%K at 9.16) has crossed above the slow line (%D at 6.34). In this, a bullish %K/%D crossover occurring below the 20-band is one of the most solid/reliable leading indicators of a trend exhaustion and possible big reversal. Whereas, the MACD line is also making bullish divergence along with the Histogram that has flipped positive (+1.679). The MACD line has crossed above the Signal line. In my stance, it is a strong signal that price (momentum) has stopped accelerating downward and is currently starting to curve upward. This bullish convergence in deep negative territory is the leading indicator of aggressive bull-market rallies.

Finally, RSI is also stably moving in the technical oversold state below 30 in years. I am observing on the left side (at the late-2022 and early-2025 market bottoms), MSFT's RSI bottomed near the ~30 line before scaling up to a multi-year bull market. So the current RSI marking of 32.05 indicates that most of the selling is done to MSFT stock price’s breaking point. All in all, MSFT stock is currently at a solid low-risk/high-reward entry point.

Alpha Protocol for Microsoft’s Next Earnings

On the Q3 earnings, I will first check out 4 important metrics, and my post-earnings stance will be based on these triggers. To begin with the first one, it is the CapEx Q/Q decline and the FCF trough. As per CFO Amy Hood, Q3 CapEx may decrease on a Q/Q basis due to the timing of cloud infrastructure buildouts and finance leases. If Q3 CapEx marks at/below $33 billion–$34 billion, it can rule out Michael Burry/the bear thesis that hyperscaler spending is an uncontrolled spiral. A Q/Q drop in CapEx can trigger a big rebound in FCF (sentimentally, in MSFT stock price). If FCF holds growth/recovery, Wall Street may re-rate MSFT from a capital-heavy utility back to a cash-deriving software leader. Further, the second factor is the Azure constant currency [CC] growth and capacity issues. On this, Q3 Azure growth is guided at 37% to 38% in CC. The bears’ argument is that Azure is losing share to Google Cloud (which grew 50% last quarter) due to OpenAI saturation. However, Microsoft argues that Azure is capacity-capped. If Azure marks higher-than-expected growth in CC. This may signal that the 1GW of capacity added in Q2 (that includes the Fairwater Atlanta/Wisconsin AI super-factories) has come online and is monetizing the RPO backlog. This positive motion can, in my opinion, trigger a big sell-side squeeze. But if the growth comes out lower, then I expect a sideways price move. To answer why so? I want to point out that Wall Street may wait for Q4 to see the opening of capacity caps.

Moreover, the third factor is M365 Copilot seat penetration and the E7 catalyst. After considering the price downtrend, in my stance, Wall Street is ignoring the (May 1, 2026) launch of the Microsoft 365 E7 Frontier Suite. On this, I will listen closely to CEO Satya Nadella's comments on forward bookings for the E7 tier. If Copilot seats go bigger (like 18 million–20 million in Q3), it may support the stance that the enterprise adoption curve is accelerating as the E7 rollout backs a large ARPU expansion in Q4-FY2026 and Q1-FY2027. Lastly, I will monitor the Windows OEM/Memory crunch impact. As per the CFO, Windows OEM revenue may decline ~10% due to the normalization of Windows 10 end-of-support and a memory pricing crunch (NAND/LPDDR costs soaring). Now considering MSFT stock price correction post-Q2, this 10% decline is already priced into MSFT stock. It is irrelevant, in my opinion, if MPC (More Personal Computing) misses a bit. Like if OEM declines by only 5% (hypothetically), it may be indicating that Apple/Android are absorbing the memory crunch’s brunt, whereas (despite that) Microsoft's PC market remains stable.

All in all, Microsoft’s business growth and stock price drop to the current technical levels is a big, strong buy (average-down long positions) tick. At a compressed 22.4x forward P/E, MSFT stock is heavily de-risked and values Microsoft as a capital-intensive hardware utility instead of a high-margin software leader. The temporary FCF trough is fully insulated by a huge contracted RPO backlog. As the heavy CapEx cycle normalizes and the massive M365 installed base upgrades to the premium E7 tier, gross margins and FCF will re-accelerate stock price toward my ~$449 blended valuation. For this long-term thesis, I will monitor four forward factors in Q3. These are Q/Q CapEx stabilization, Azure constant currency growth over threshold, the acceleration of Copilot paid seats beyond the current penetration, and Microsoft Foundry's progress in diversifying enterprise inference workloads away from OpenAI to reduce counterparty risk.

data.title: AI's Hidden Cost: Why Water Risk Belongs On Every Investor's Radar
data.description:
By Sara Rosner | James Russo | Henna Nordqvist, CFA

Access to clean water may be a deciding factor in who wins or loses the AI race.

AI’s rapid growth is driving demand not only for electricity but also for the clean water needed to run its physical infrastructure. As data centers expand, rising water intensity is straining supplies and testing long-term sustainability. In our analysis, these pressures create both risks and opportunities for active investors.

How Cool Is AI?

AI’s explosive growth shows little sign of ebbing, with a record $350 billion projected for AI-focused business investment in 2025 alone. As a result, global capacity for AI’s server-housing facilities, or data centers, is expected to rise 23% in each of the next five years, with the US dominating.

The build-out is expected to spike data center electricity demand by 160% in the US, contributing to a 25% increase in overall power demand through 2030, according to the International Energy Agency. Meeting greater global power needs will have distinct challenges. But we also see a variety of potential risks stemming from water becoming either more scarce or less pure.

This is because AI’s powerful data centers tend to run extra hot, and many of them require a lot of ultrapure water to constantly chill them. Consequently, a data center’s success will likely depend as much on access to ample and clean water as on reliable power sources.

Rising Water Risk as a Market Disrupter

Data centers aren’t the only AI boom participants in search of water. Semiconductor manufacturers and utilities are also intensive water users and are likely to need even more of it (Display). US power generation—particularly coal and nuclear—accounts for about 70% of all freshwater withdrawals, though most is returned to its source after cooling.

US Water Demand Among AI Boom Participants Is Rising Rapidly

Research we conducted with the Columbia Climate School shows that these three areas combined are expected to grow demand for clean water by 33% through 2030. Location can also add material risk, since many data centers are either situated in or planned for regions already stressed for water, our findings show.

We see a continued ramp-up in data center build-out, chip production and power generation in the near term. And water risk is emerging as a key constraint for these and other industries—potentially more disruptive than climate change itself. For instance, a Bloomberg study found that about $70 trillion in global GDP could be directly exposed to high water stress by 2050.

We’re already seeing companies change up their long-term playbooks due to near-term water concerns—Constellation Brands’ now-abandoned beer plant in Mexico and Google’s nixed plans for a data center in Chile, for example.

That’s why we believe water stewardship is integral to active investment selection. By the same token, company engagement is crucial to determining a business’s water-risk exposure and whether it’s helping to solve the problem for its own benefit and, in some cases, for others.

Assessing a Company’s Chill Factor

Many companies outside of the tech industry—whether household names or unfamiliar start-ups—are participating in the AI boom in some fashion. But we think progress will come more readily to innovators that share lasting solutions to AI’s biggest challenges, including water scarcity (Display).

Water Risks Can Lead to Investment Opportunities Across the AI Build-Out

We see a significant milestone in harnessing the ocean for cooling solutions. Though costlier than purifying water from municipal sources, desalinating seawater is very effective in semiconductor fabrication. The process uses either evaporation or membrane filters to remove minerals that harm equipment and microchips. Companies such as DuPont Water Solutions, LG Chem and Flowserve are among the key enablers in this growing market, which is expected to top $50 billion by 2032.

Direct-to-chip liquid cooling also has potential in a water-stressed world. This method places metal cooling plates in direct contact with processors to dissipate heat while low amounts of water carry the heat off through grooves. Asetek is one of the leading public companies in this space, which is currently dotted with private firms.

Yet another innovation is closed-cycle cooling, which is highly water efficient and environmentally friendly. The process lowers heat by circulating liquid coolant—mostly water—throughout a system that can range from evaporative towers to condensers. SPX Technologies stands out as a global pioneer in the public markets. However, numerous privately held companies are also leading advancement in this space, such as Kelvion and Hamon Group.

Global water scarcity isn’t a risk unique to AI, but the race for AI domination is increasingly revealing its hidden dangers and potential costs. And with so much of today’s market value tied to a handful of AI leaders, responsible investing requires recognizing water-risk exposure at every level—as well as the opportunities created by it.

The authors would like to thank Maxwell Lulavy, Responsible Investing Research Analyst at AB, for his significant contributions to the research behind this blog.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and should not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P. It should not be assumed that investments in the securities mentioned have necessarily been or will necessarily be profitable.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oil Shock: Will The Fed Intervene (Part 2)
data.description:
Last week, we discussed the risk of an oil shock leading to a recession. To wit:

“After more than three decades of watching oil markets upend economies, one pattern keeps repeating: investors learn the wrong lessons from the last shock. The 1973 OPEC embargo taught us that geopolitical disruptions are temporary. That lesson then got everyone killed, financially speaking, in 1979. The 2003 Iraq War produced only a mild oil bump and no recession, so traders got comfortable. Then 2008 happened. Today, with Brent crude having spiked over 60 percent since U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran began in late February, the same dangerous reasoning is circulating again. That narrative is that this ‘event” is manageable and will resolve quickly. If that is the case, then the economy will absorb it.

That may indeed be the case. However, the conditions that determine whether an oil shock becomes a full recession are specific, quantifiable, and worth examining with clear eyes. That is what this analysis does.”

That article digs into the plumbing behind oil shocks and recession and exposes why, over the years, I’ve learned to distrust the loudest voices in the room. Right now, some of the most prominent macro commentators, the “Persistent Purveyors of Doom,” are making a variation of the same argument: the Strait of Hormuz closure is not merely a serious risk of an oil shock and recession; it’s the beginning of the end. Markets will crash within weeks. Stocking up on essentials is the rational response. The global financial system, already fragile, cannot survive what’s coming.

I certainly can understand the appeal of that thesis. The disruption is genuinely unprecedented. Roughly 20% of global oil and LNG now sit stranded behind a military blockade. Furthermore, the secondary effects on fertilizer prices, food inflation, and petrochemical supply chains are real and compounding. This is, as they say, “not nothing.” But ‘this is serious’ and ‘this is the worst crisis in anyone’s lifetime’ are two very different claims. The evidence, as we will discuss today, supports the first one far more than the second.

The catastrophist argument rests on a critical hidden assumption: that the global economy, central banks, and governments sit passively while a supply shock plays out. History doesn’t support that assumption — not once.

In 1973, the Arab oil embargo removed roughly 6% of global oil supplies from the market. Painful, yes. Civilizational collapse, no. Markets adjusted, alternative suppliers ramped up, and policymakers, however clumsily, responded. In 1990, Iraq’s invasion of Kuwait spiked oil prices by nearly 80% in three months. Within six months, prices had reversed almost entirely as supply alternatives emerged and the military situation resolved. Even in 2008, when oil hit $147 per barrel, the story wasn’t that oil destroyed the financial system. The financial system had already built its own bomb. Oil just lit the fuse faster.

What we’re seeing today follows the same pattern. The IEA has already authorized the release of 400 million barrels from strategic reserves, the largest emergency release in history. Saudi Arabia rerouted production through the East-West pipeline to Yanbu port on the Red Sea. The U.S. military launched an active campaign to reopen the strait on March 19th. These are not the actions of a world sitting passively in the path of an unstoppable freight train. They’re the messy, imperfect, and historically consistent responses of a system under stress. More crucially, it is doing what systems under stress do: adapting.

The Real Risk: The Sequencing Trap

Here’s what the catastrophists get right, though it’s not the story they’re telling. The genuine danger isn’t a sudden market collapse in three weeks. It’s the sequencing trap that every major oil shock has sprung, and the Federal Reserve is already caught inside it.

Oil shocks are simultaneously inflationary and recessionary. That’s what makes them so insidious and so difficult to manage. In the short run, energy prices spike, headline inflation surges, and the Fed cannot ease. But elevated energy costs act as a regressive tax on every consumer regardless of income. Disposable income gets crushed, business input costs compress margins, and hiring freezes give way to layoffs. Eventually, consumer demand collapses faster than supply adjusts, and at that point, the inflationary impulse reverses hard.

Has such a cycle occurred previously? Yes, in 1974, 1980, 1982, and in 2008, when oil at $147 in June had fallen to $32 by December. The self-limiting nature of oil shocks is one of the most reliable patterns in macroeconomic history. The inflation doesn’t last. The recession that follows it does.

That’s why positioning right now matters enormously. Ben Bernanke’s QE doctrine worked brilliantly in 2008-09, 2011, and again in March 2020 because each episode shared a common condition: a demand-side deflationary shock. Credit froze, spending collapsed, and inflation fell. The Fed could inject liquidity without stoking inflation because there was no inflation to stoke.

A supply-side stagflationary shock inverts that logic entirely. Aggressive rate cuts into $120 oil weaken the dollar, making energy priced in dollars more expensive for U.S. consumers. QE signals panic and risks re-igniting the wage-price spiral the Fed spent 2022 and 2023 fighting to break. Arthur Burns made exactly this mistake in the 1970s, and the result wasn’t stabilization. It was stagflation that took Paul Volcker and 20% interest rates to unwind. That institutional memory lives in every Fed governor’s DNA right now.

To be fair, today’s Fed operates from a meaningfully stronger position than Burns did. The U.S. economy is roughly 60% less energy-intensive per dollar of GDP than it was in 1973, and American shale production has transformed the country into a net energy exporter. That one fact is a structural buffer that simply didn’t exist when the Arab embargo hit. Those differences reduce the severity of the transmission from high oil prices into core inflation, and they give the Fed slightly more room to maneuver before a full-blown wage-price spiral takes hold. What those differences don’t change, however, is the fundamental sequencing dynamic. That is, a sustained supply shock still produces the same inflationary-then-recessionary arc, and the Fed still cannot ease aggressively until inflation visibly cracks. However, when that moment arrives, it will be with somewhat less economic damage already baked in.

Yet the Fed isn’t completely paralyzed. Targeted liquidity facilities, repo operations, and coordinated dollar swap lines with the ECB and BOJ can prevent a credit market seizure without the inflationary optics of full QE.

The Fed will provide plumbing support. The only question is whether it has political cover to do more, and with oil near $120, it almost certainly does not. At least, not yet.

Three Scenarios and What They Mean for Your Portfolio

The specific outcome depends almost entirely on how long the Strait of Hormuz disruption persists. Based on what we know as of March 29 — the U.S. military campaign underway since March 19, Iran’s leadership structure fractured, but mines still in the water and attacks continuing — here is how I’d assign the probabilities across the three paths. Let me walk through each in detail, including what I expect from Treasury yields, the dollar, and equities in each case.

SCENARIO ONE — 50% Probability: Resolution within six to eight weeks. This remains the base case, though it’s a coin flip rather than a certainty. The U.S. military campaign, which has been underway since March 19th, is the dominant variable — Iran’s strategic position is deteriorating by the week, its economy is imploding, its leadership structure is fractured, and it has no viable endgame that involves a prolonged closure. Historical precedent also favors shorter disruptions. The 50% odds reflect the fact that mine clearance and war-risk insurance reinstatement add weeks even after hostilities formally subside. A combination of diplomatic pressure, military progress, and economic pain on all sides produces a partial reopening.

  • Oil retreats from the current $120 range toward $80 to $90.
  • The Fed cuts two to three times in the second half of 2026, framing it as normalization rather than a crisis response.
  • Treasury yields move from 4.4% to 3.8%-4.0% as recession fears moderate.
  • The dollar weakens 3% to 5% on the DXY.
  • Equities recover but remain 8% to 12% below pre-conflict highs through year-end as multiple compression persists.

SCENARIO TWO — 35% Probability: Disruption persists through Q3 2026. The second most likely outcome. Even a nominal ceasefire doesn’t instantly reopen commercial shipping. Things such as war-risk insurance, mine sweeping, and shipper confidence all take time to restore. A grinding, partial resolution that keeps oil elevated and supply chains stressed through summer is entirely plausible, particularly if Iran’s remnant forces pursue asymmetric harassment rather than formal closure. Oil stays in the $100 to $120 range for two full quarters. Demand destruction compounds into a genuine recession. Headline inflation initially stays elevated, but core starts rolling over as consumer spending collapses. By September or October, the Fed faces the sequencing trap in full: an undeniable recession, inflation reversing, and financial conditions tightening into the downturn.

  • The Fed cuts aggressively, 150 to 200 basis points in rapid succession, restarting QE and framing it as financial stability support.
  • The 10-year Treasury yield falls toward 3.0%-3.2%.
  • The dollar surges by 5% to 8% on safe-haven demand before weakening sharply once QE restarts.
  • The S&P 500 tests the 4,800 to 5,000 range, a 20% to 25% decline before the Fed backstop stabilizes sentiment.

SCENARIO THREE — 15% Probability: Extended closure triggers a credit event. This is a genuine tail risk, not a rounding error, and the 15% odds reflect that. The private credit, leveraged loan, and commercial real estate stress has been building since the 2022 rate cycle finally fully cracked under the combined weight of the energy shock and prolonged elevated rates. A major credit vehicle or institution fails. Suddenly, it’s not just a recession, it’s a credit seizure.

  • The Fed cuts to 0% and launches a full QE program simultaneously.
  • Treasury yields collapse toward 2.5% to 2.8%.
  • The dollar surges 8% to 12% in the acute phase before a sustained multi-quarter reversal as the balance sheet expands.
  • Equities decline more sharply as forward earnings estimates are slashed (roughly 40-50%), before recovering sharply once intervention takes hold. The S&P 500 could conceivably test the 3,800 to 4,200 range at the trough.

The Critical Takeaway For Investors: Scenario 3 has only a 15% probability but could imply a 40–50% drawdown in equities. That’s an expected loss contribution too large to ignore, even in a base-case world. Therefore, we continue to maintain a defensive posture regardless of which scenario ultimately plays out.

What Investors Should Do Right Now

I have lived and worked through four major market crises in my career. Over that time, I’ve repeatedly watched two destructive instincts play out with near-perfect reliability. The first is panic-selling everything at the worst possible moment. The second is dismissing real risk because the sky hasn’t fallen yet. Both have cost investors dearly, and neither is the right posture here.

The evidence of previous oil shocks supports a deliberate defensive shift for the time being. Increasing cash levels, holding short-duration treasuries, and reducing exposure to more globally cyclical equities. In other words, the “reflation trade” that began the year has likely come to an abrupt end. The sequencing trap is real, and the Fed will likely be slower to arrive than bulls are pricing unless the inflationary impulse reverses rapidly. However, in reality, the gap between the inflationary phase and the Fed pivot is the danger zone. That is potentially two to four quarters of deteriorating growth, declining earnings, and no central bank backstop, leaving plenty of room for portfolio damage.

Yes, the Fed will eventually ride to the rescue, and history makes that impossible to ignore. The question is always the same: how much permanent capital loss accumulates before that pivot arrives?

What is most crucial to your outcomes is not letting the “doom crowd” paralyze you. However, don’t let the optimists convince you that the risks of an oil shock are already priced in. The damage in a sequencing trap happens quietly, over quarters, not in a sudden crash that gives you time to react. The time to position defensively is today, not after the recession data confirms what the oil market is already telling you.

“Defense over offense — and trade accordingly.”

data.title: Snowflake: This Company Isn't SaaS, Calmly 'Buy' As Others Panic Sell
data.description:
If there's one trait as an investor that I think is more important than any other, it's the ability and willingness to change our minds as situations move. I'm not permanently bullish or permanently bearish on any stock. It all depends on where the stock is trading today and how its fundamentals are performing relative to that valuation.

I was deeply bearish on Snowflake Inc. (SNOW), the cloud data warehousing company, as recently as last year, when the stock was flirting with levels just shy of $300. Today, the SaaSpocalypse has put a massive dent into this stock, bringing it down ~40% since the start of the year and wiping off tens of billions in market value. The key question now is, when will the pain end?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Snowflake in December, when the stock had first cratered to the low $200s. I'll admit that I wasn't predicting the so-called "SaaSpocalypse" and that I pulled the "Buy" trigger too early. At the same time, when a stock nosedives this deeply, it's more than appropriate to take a fresh look, and I see no red flags that warrant the massive erosion in Snowflake's multiples. In the span of just a few quarters, Snowflake has made the tremendous journey from significantly overvalued to visibly undervalued, leading me to reiterate my "Buy" rating on the company.

It's impossible to discuss Snowflake without discussing the notion of the SaaSpocalypse itself. To put it into a nutshell, I do believe AI will have an outsized impact on the software sector, but the way in which it will impact incumbent players depends heavily on the type of software and the pricing model.

Complex, data-heavy, and mission-critical software platforms that run daily company operations are unlikely to be ripped out. As are software companies that serve large corporations, particularly those in regulated industries. Where AI will have the biggest impact is indirectly through layoffs. Oracle (ORCL) made waves by laying off ~30,000 employees; Meta recently announced plans to remove ~20% of its staffers, and Block has already begun slicing down its headcount by 40%. This heavily impacts software companies whose business models rest on a per-head seat count. Previously, the market had thought these business models to have a deep moat, with companies unlikely to terminate seats.

From the beginning, Snowflake has insisted that it is not a SaaS company. The company no longer includes this slide in its quarterly investor presentations, but as recently as the first quarter of FY23, the company clarified that its business doesn't rely on seat counts but is entirely dependent on customers' data consumption.

The same is true today. The slide below, contained in the company's most recent investor presentation, showcases its billing model today. Snowflake bills its customers based on compute, storage, and data transfer. It bills upfront, while revenue is recognized as customers actually use the Snowflake platform on a day-to-day basis.

From a practical standpoint, what this means is that Snowflake will be largely unaffected by mass layoffs (like at Oracle and Meta), provided that customers still use the same amount of data stored on Snowflake platforms. In fact, the huge data center capex boom and the fact that AI is unleashing a brand new wave of startups only point to data consumption increasing rather than decreasing.

In my view, Snowflake's selloff is a mere misunderstanding by the market of how AI will impact different software companies, and Snowflake's non-SaaS, consumption-based business model shields it from downside. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

My assertion that the "SaaSpocalypse" won't cause severe harm to Snowflake's business isn't just conjecture, but it's readily observed in Snowflake's most recent data. Take a look at the Q4 earnings summary below:

Snowflake's revenue grew 30% y/y to $1.28 billion, outstripping Wall Street's expectations of $1.25 billion (+27% y/y) by a three-point margin. We note as well that revenue growth accelerated slightly from 29% y/y growth in Q3.

This is just one of several indicators that AI isn't acting as a net headwind to Snowflake. We find as well that the company's net revenue retention rates remained steady at 125%. This indicates that the average Snowflake customer is boosting its spending on the Snowflake platform by 25%. This helps to justify the notion that companies' desire to deploy new applications, particularly buoyed by AI, relies on reams of data, which is precisely what drives Snowflake's business model.

Also, Snowflake is signing a greater chunk of long-term backlog. Its overall remaining performance obligations (RPO, which is essentially backlog) grew 42% y/y to $9.77 billion, far outstripping revenue growth. We note that Snowflake guided to $5.7 billion in product revenue for FY27, so the company's current backlog indicates that it has just shy of two years of revenue already contractually committed.

Again, if customers were looking to decommission Snowflake, replace it with AI solutions, or slice down their spending on the platform, we wouldn't be observing such a large increase in contractual backlog.

Speaking to large deals on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call, CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy commented as follows:

Bookings and multiyear contracts are a clear indication of the trust that our partners have in their future with Snowflake. And yes, the product acceleration and velocity goes a lot towards convincing customers that we are a platform for the future. We didn't do anything particularly special in the quarters. Yes, we did adjust the compensation plan to also take bookings into account last year. But in many ways, that represents a reversion back to how things were 2 years ago. And we plan to continue that this year. So it's very much business as usual. I do think that the $400 million, $400-plus million deal that we signed is an indication of the importance that we deliver to that large financial services customers. We have previously talked about deals in the $250 million range. I think it represents a maturity of Snowflake as a durable provider, not just today of data services, but also into the future.

The company also continues to scale profitably, with pro forma operating margins of 11%, improving 2 points y/y and still positioning Snowflake as a "Rule of 40" software company.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

At current share prices near $134, Snowflake's market cap has fallen to $46.30 billion. Netting off the substantial $4.78 billion of cash against $2.28 billion of debt (a $2.50 billion net cash position) on Snowflake's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $43.80 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Snowflake ending in January 2027), Snowflake has guided to 27% y/y revenue growth to $5.66 billion in product revenue. Consensus is calling for $5.92 billion in total revenue (including professional services), or 26% y/y growth. The company's expectation of a 23% FCF margin also implies ~$1.36 billion in free cash flow against this revenue profile.

Snowflake trades at:

  • 7.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 32.2x EV/FY27 FCF

Though not a bargain-basement stock per se, we note that Snowflake trades reasonably for a "Rule of 40" software stock that is enjoying consumption-based AI tailwinds, as evidenced by the recent acceleration in growth rates plus the buildup in long-term backlog. Also of note is the fact that Snowflake is expecting to reduce stock-based compensation as a percentage of revenue to 27% in FY27, from 34% in FY26 and 41% in FY25 - addressing a key concern many investors have about dilution with Snowflake and other software companies (with share prices declining much deeper after Snowflake's earnings release, we think stock compensation levels could fall even further this year).

Of course, there are risks here to keep in mind. The macro remains unstable, and across the software sector, many companies are reporting slower deal cycles. And amid foundational AI companies striking large deals with cloud providers like Oracle for data center capacity, we could see heightened competition for data warehousing against larger players.

That said, I think Snowflake's huge slide from peaks gives us a de-risked entry point into this stock. Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.
